Tennis News

5 Tips for the Wimbledon Ballot and When to Use Resale Sites

ByChloe Drummond|12 February 2025  ·  4 min read

Centre Court at Wimbledon remains the most prestigious tennis venue in the world. (Getty Images)

Missed the Wimbledon ballot? Don't panic. We explain when the secondary market becomes your best option and how to avoid overpaying.

How the Wimbledon Public Ballot Actually Works

The All England Club's public ballot opens each year between 30 July and 31 December for the following summer's Championships. Applicants must register individually — joint applications are not permitted — and successful ballots are announced the following April. Each successful applicant receives a maximum of two tickets for a single day session on Centre Court, Court 1, or Court 2. The ballot is heavily oversubscribed: in recent years fewer than 1 in 12 applicants have been successful, which is why so many fans turn to the secondary market.

Five Tips to Maximise Your Ballot Chances

First, register early — the system confirms each registration by email and early registrants have historically had a marginally higher success rate. Second, use a personal email address you check regularly, as ballot results are dispatched quickly and unclaimed allocations are re-drawn. Third, apply for grounds passes as a backup — they are separate from the Centre Court ballot and often overlooked. Fourth, check that your postal address is correct; mismatched addresses have caused tickets to be issued and not received. Fifth, register every year: the ballot is random but persistent applicants statistically receive tickets every five to seven years.

When the Secondary Market Is Your Best Option

If you miss the ballot, resale platforms become essential. The official Wimbledon Debenture Holders scheme releases a small number of Centre Court tickets through the official resale portal, and these carry a price premium but are guaranteed authentic. For broader availability, FootballTicketNet and Awin both list verified e-transfer tickets with buyer guarantees. Research shows that prices for Centre Court day sessions drop by an average of 18% in the 48 hours after a high-profile player is eliminated — so flexibility to watch the tournament rather than a specific match pays off handsomely.

Queue Tickets: The Hidden Gem Most Fans Miss

The famous Wimbledon Queue offers approximately 500 Centre Court tickets and 500 Court 1 tickets each day through the physical queuing system. Grounds passes, which grant access to the outside courts, are also available and provide an atmospheric and affordable way to experience the Championships. Arriving before 6 am dramatically improves your chances of securing Centre Court queue tickets; many seasoned fans camp overnight. The Queue is free to join and stewarded by the All England Club, making it one of the safest and most authentic ways to experience Wimbledon without paying secondary market prices.
